section.title: Wisdom and Folly
section.sectionRationale: The tenth discourse closes the extended teaching of chapters 1 to 9 with two competing invitations to a feast. Wisdom builds a house and calls the simple to her table, verses 1 to 6, framing reflections on mockers and the wise around the book's motto restated, verses 7 to 12, before Folly builds a rival, empty invitation of her own, verses 13 to 18.
section.thesis: Two women call from their houses to the same simple passers by, Wisdom, offering a feast that leads to life and understanding, and Folly, offering stolen pleasure that leads to death, and every person must choose which table to sit at.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Wisdom's house, feast, and invitation, verses 1 to 6; reflections on mockers, the wise, and the restated motto of the book, verses 7 to 12; and Folly's rival house and empty invitation, verses 13 to 18.

verses.text: {{chokmah|Wisdom}} has built her house; she has carved out her seven pillars.
She has prepared her meat and mixed her wine; she has also set her table.
She has sent out her maidservants; she calls out from the heights of the city.
"Whoever is simple, let him turn in here!" she says to him who lacks judgment.
"Come, eat my bread and drink the wine I have mixed.
Leave your folly behind, and you will live; walk in the way of understanding."

units.label: Mockers, the wise, and the book's motto restated
units.note: the fear of the LORD, again

verses.text: He who corrects a mocker brings shame on himself; he who rebukes a wicked man taints himself.
Do not rebuke a mocker, or he will hate you; rebuke a wise man, and he will love you.
Instruct a wise man, and he will be wiser still; teach a righteous man, and he will increase his learning.
The {{yirah|fear of the LORD}} is the beginning of wisdom, and knowledge of the Holy One is understanding.
For through wisdom your days will be multiplied, and years will be added to your life.
If you are wise, you are wise to your own advantage; but if you scoff, you alone will bear the consequences.
The woman named {{kesiluth|Folly}} is loud; she is naive and knows nothing.
She sits at the door of her house, on a seat in the heights of the city,
calling out to those who pass by, who make their paths straight.
"Whoever is simple, let him turn in here!" she says to him who lacks judgment.
"Stolen water is sweet, and bread eaten in secret is tasty!"
But they do not know that the dead are there, that her guests are in the depths of Sheol.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, pictured here as a gracious hostess who builds a substantial house, prepares a full feast, and sends servants to call the simple to her table freely. Her invitation costs the guest nothing but is bought at her own expense, offering life and understanding to anyone who will come.

yirah.gloss: The fear of the LORD, restated here in verse 10 in almost the same words as the book's opening motto in 1:7, forming bookends around the whole first section of Proverbs and confirming that everything taught in chapters 1 to 9 flows from this one root.

kesiluth.gloss: Folly, personified as a rival woman who mimics Wisdom's invitation with an almost identical call, whoever is simple, let him turn in here, but offers only stolen pleasure that conceals a house full of the dead.

basic.context: This chapter closes the long teaching section of Proverbs 1 to 9 with a vivid contrast. Wisdom builds a grand house, prepares a real feast, and sends servants to invite the simple in, promising life and understanding to anyone who comes. Folly mimics this same invitation almost word for word, but her house hides death, and her guests do not realize until too late that they have joined the dead in Sheol. In between stands a reflection on how mockers and the wise respond so differently to correction, anchored by the restatement of the book's opening motto, the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.
basic.hermeneutics: The deliberate echo between Wisdom's invitation, verse 4, and Folly's invitation, verse 16, whoever is simple, let him turn in here, is the key to reading this chapter. Both call to the same undecided people; the difference is entirely in what lies behind the door. This teaches that temptation rarely looks obviously evil, it imitates the language of what is good, and only the fear of the LORD, restated at the chapter's center, verse 10, equips a person to tell the two houses apart.
basic.originalLanguages: Chokmah, wisdom, here builds and hosts like a substantial noblewoman, sparing no expense on her seven pillared house and prepared feast. The chapter's center restates yirah, the fear of the LORD, in language nearly identical to 1:7, binding the whole first section of the book together around this one motto. And kesiluth, folly, is personified as wisdom's dark twin, using the very same words of invitation to lure the same simple passers by toward death instead of life.
basic.typology: Wisdom's free invitation, eat my bread and drink the wine I have mixed, verse 5, anticipates the gospel's own invitation to a costly feast freely offered, most directly Isaiah's call, come, buy wine and milk without money, Isaiah 55:1, and Christ's own words, I am the bread of life, John 6:35.
basic.christ: Wisdom's invitation in this chapter, come, eat my bread and drink the wine I have mixed, and leave your folly behind and live, is a striking foreshadowing of Christ's own invitation. He identifies Himself as the bread of life, John 6:35, and offers His body and blood at a table prepared at great cost to Him, so that all who come, however simple or lacking in judgment, might truly live. Where Wisdom's house in Proverbs is one image among several, in Christ the invitation, the host, and the feast itself are united in a single person.
basic.god: God, through Wisdom's picture, is generous and hospitable, sparing no expense to prepare a feast and going out of His way to call in the simple and undiscerning, not only the already wise. He is also just, allowing genuine consequences to fall on those who choose the rival house, so that scoffers alone bear what their own choice brings, verse 12.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 55:1 to 2 issues a strikingly similar invitation, come, buy wine and milk without money, echoing Wisdom's free feast. John 6:35 has Christ call Himself the bread of life. Matthew 22:1 to 14 pictures the kingdom as a wedding feast to which all are invited. Revelation 19:9 calls the redeemed blessed who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb.
basic.oneStory: Two houses, two feasts, two invitations using almost the same words, this chapter's picture captures the whole of life's central choice, and the whole Bible's central invitation. That invitation reaches its fullest form in Christ, who offers Himself as the bread of life at a table prepared at His own cost, calling even the simple and undiscerning to leave folly behind and live.

context.literary: The final discourse of chapters 1 to 9, structured as a matching pair of invitation scenes, Wisdom's house and feast, verses 1 to 6, and Folly's house and feast, verses 13 to 18, framing a central reflection on mockers, the wise, and the fear of the LORD, verses 7 to 12, which restates the book's opening motto and closes the introductory section of Proverbs.
context.historical: Public invitations issued by servants calling from the heights of the city, verse 3, reflect real ancient practice, where a wealthy host might send servants into the streets to gather guests for a banquet, a custom Jesus Himself draws on in His parable of the great banquet, Luke 14:16 to 24.
context.canonical: Verse 10 restates 1:7 almost word for word, forming a literary bracket, an inclusio, around the entire first section of the book, confirming that the fear of the LORD is not a passing remark but the controlling principle of everything taught in chapters 1 through 9.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To close the book's introductory discourses with one final, unmistakable choice, pictured as two competing feasts calling to the same undecided passer by, so that the reader understands every subsequent proverb as an outworking of which house, wisdom's or folly's, one has chosen to enter.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The invitations themselves are extended as genuine appeals, come, eat, leave your folly and live, making this passage directly prescriptive, choose Wisdom's house. The reflections on mockers and the wise, verses 7 to 9, describe a general pattern of how each type typically responds to correction, informing prudent practice, rebuking a mocker tends to provoke hatred, rather than issuing an absolute rule that correction of the openly rebellious should never be attempted.

debates.question: Does verse 8, 'do not rebuke a mocker, or he will hate you,' forbid ever confronting scoffers?

views.label: A general principle of prudence, not an absolute rule
views.case: Proverbs elsewhere commends rebuking fools and even the prophets confronted hardened mockers directly; this verse describes the typical, predictable reaction of a settled mocker to correction, counseling wisdom in when and how to engage, not silence toward sin.

views.label: A caution against wasted, provocative confrontation
views.case: The verse's practical wisdom is that confronting someone already committed to mockery usually produces only hostility and no change, so energy is better spent instructing the wise, who will actually receive it, verse 9.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the verse is descriptive wisdom about likely outcomes, not a categorical ban on ever addressing sin; it commends discernment about when confrontation is likely to bear fruit and cautions against needless conflict with those who have already hardened into scoffing.

study.originalLanguages: Chokmah, wisdom, hosts like a noblewoman of means, her seven pillared house and prepared feast signaling abundance and permanence. The chapter's center restates yirah, the fear of the LORD, verse 10, in language nearly identical to the book's opening motto, 1:7, deliberately bracketing the whole introductory section. And kesiluth, folly, mimics wisdom's very words of invitation, verse 16, exposing how temptation so often wears the same clothing as what is good, differing only in what it truly delivers.

typology.type: Wisdom's feast, eat my bread and drink the wine I have mixed, Proverbs 9:5
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the bread of life, who offers Himself at a table prepared at His own cost, John 6:35, Luke 22:19-20.

study.christ: Wisdom's invitation to a feast she has prepared, come, eat my bread and drink the wine I have mixed, and leave your folly behind and live, verses 5 to 6, anticipates Christ's own self identification as the bread of life, John 6:35, and His invitation to a table He prepared at the cost of His own body and blood. Where Wisdom's house in Proverbs offers one image of life among several in the book, Christ Himself is both the host and the feast, and His invitation, like Wisdom's, still goes out to the simple and those lacking judgment, calling them to leave folly behind and truly live.
study.god: God, pictured through Wisdom's hospitality, spares no expense to prepare a feast and actively seeks out the simple and undiscerning rather than waiting passively for the already wise. He is equally just, ensuring that those who choose folly's rival table bear the real and lasting consequences of that choice, even though her invitation looked, on the surface, just as appealing as Wisdom's own.

crossReferences.note: Come, buy wine and milk without money, an invitation strikingly similar to Wisdom's free feast in verses 1-6.
Christ declares Himself the bread of life, echoing and fulfilling Wisdom's invitation to eat her bread.
The parable of the great banquet, where servants are sent to call in guests, echoing the sending of Wisdom's maidservants in verse 3.
Blessed are those invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb, the ultimate feast this chapter's imagery anticipates.

study.oneStory: This closing chapter of Proverbs 1 to 9 pictures the whole of life as a choice between two houses calling with nearly identical words, one leading to a feast of life, one hiding a house full of the dead. That choice runs through the whole Bible until it reaches Christ, who offers Himself as the true bread and wine of a feast prepared at His own cost, still calling the simple and undiscerning to leave folly behind and live.

quiz.q: What has Wisdom done to prepare for her guests, according to verses 1 to 5?

options.text: Built a substantial house, prepared a full feast, and sent servants to call in the simple.
options.note: All three details, house, feast, and servants sent to invite, appear directly in the text.

options.text: Hidden herself away from public view.
options.note: The passage stresses her public, active invitation, the opposite of hiding.

options.text: Charged a high entrance fee for her feast.
options.note: The passage presents her feast as an open invitation, not a paid entry.

options.text: Invited only the already wise and accomplished.
options.note: The text specifically targets 'whoever is simple' and 'him who lacks judgment,' not only the already wise.

quiz.q: What does verse 10 restate, and where else does this appear?

options.text: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom, echoing the book's opening motto in 1:7.
options.note: This near identical restatement forms a literary bracket around chapters 1 to 9.

options.text: A completely new teaching never mentioned before in the book.
options.note: It is a deliberate restatement of the book's opening motto, not a new idea.

options.text: A warning specific to kings alone.
options.note: The statement is general, addressed to any reader, not restricted to kings.

options.text: A minor detail with little significance to the book's structure.
options.note: This understates its role; the near verbatim repetition deliberately brackets the entire first section of Proverbs.

quiz.q: How does Folly's invitation in verse 16 compare to Wisdom's in verse 4?

options.text: It uses almost identical words, showing that temptation often mimics what is good rather than looking obviously evil.
options.note: Both verses use the same phrase, 'Whoever is simple, let him turn in here,' making this parallel deliberate.

options.text: It is obviously different and easy to distinguish at a glance.
options.note: The passage's power lies precisely in how similar the two invitations sound.

options.text: It targets a completely different audience.
options.note: Both invitations target the same simple, undiscerning passers by.

options.text: It offers a more expensive and lavish feast than Wisdom's.
options.note: Folly's offering is described as stolen and secret, not lavish or costly like Wisdom's prepared feast.

quiz.q: What is the hidden reality behind Folly's house, according to verse 18?

options.text: Her guests do not realize that the dead are there, in the depths of Sheol.
options.note: This is the chapter's final, sobering revelation.

options.text: Her house is simply empty and harmless.
options.note: The passage explicitly describes it as hiding death, not harmlessness.

options.text: Her guests become wealthy and secure.
options.note: The passage's outcome is death, not wealth or security.

options.text: Her house offers temporary pleasure with no lasting effect either way.
options.note: The passage insists the effect is lasting and fatal, not neutral or temporary.

quiz.q: How does Wisdom's feast in this chapter anticipate Christ?

options.text: Christ calls Himself the bread of life and offers a table prepared at the cost of His own body and blood.
options.note: John 6:35 and the Last Supper directly echo this chapter's image of a costly, life giving feast freely offered.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament at all.
options.note: The invitation and bread and wine imagery are picked up directly in the Gospels.

options.text: It shows that Christ's invitation is only for the already wise.
options.note: Both Wisdom's invitation and Christ's own ministry specifically target the simple, the lost, and those lacking, not only the wise.

options.text: It refers only to a literal historical banquet with no further significance.
options.note: This underplays the deliberate, developed connection Scripture draws between this feast and the gospel's invitation to Christ's table.

questions: Wisdom's invitation and Folly's invitation use almost identical words. What temptations in your life look, on the surface, just as appealing as something genuinely good?
Wisdom spares no expense to prepare her feast and call in the simple. Where do you see God's generosity toward you, even before you had anything to offer in return?
Verse 10 restates the fear of the LORD as the beginning of wisdom, exactly as in chapter 1. Has this truth become more or less central to how you actually make decisions since you first learned it?
Folly's guests do not realize the dead are in her house until it is too late. What warning signs are you tempted to ignore because the immediate pleasure feels sweet, like stolen water, verse 17?

Christ invites the simple and those lacking judgment to His own table, prepared at His own cost. What would it look like to come to Him honestly today, without pretending to already be wise or capable on your own?

translationNotes.note: Some ancient versions, the Septuagint, Syriac, and Aramaic Targum, read 'set up' rather than 'built,' a minor variation that does not change the overall picture of Wisdom's established house.
Some ancient versions read 'through Me' rather than 'through wisdom,' making Wisdom's own voice, rather than the abstract idea, the direct speaker of this promise.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 9:1-18. No em or en dashes present in this passage; none used anywhere in the file.
